actions.thought: I hold AdTh on the Button (Small Blind) in a heads-up match, which is a strong starting hand well above the average. Being in position post-flop is a significant advantage. With 100BB stacks, raising is the standard play to build the pot and seize the initiative. I will open with a standard min-raise to 200, which offers a good risk-to-reward ratio and puts pressure on the Big Blind.

QdJh is a strong Broadway hand that easily defends against a 2x button raise. Given the 3:1 pot odds, calling is the standard play to see a flop. While a 3-bet is possible, calling keeps the pot manageable out of position and allows the hand to realize its equity against the button's wide opening range.

On this Ace-high flop, the board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. My hand, QdJh, has completely missed the flop, offering only very thin backdoor potential with the Jack of hearts. As the Big Blind, the standard defensive play is to check to the aggressor and see how they react. I have no reason to lead into the raiser on this texture.

I hold Top Pair with a good kicker (AdTh) on an Ace-high board (Ah 8h 5d). This board texture strongly favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor. In a heads-up pot, Top Pair is a very strong hand that merits extracting value from worse Aces (A2-A9), middle/bottom pairs (8x, 5x), and flush/straight draws. Checking back is an option for pot control, but betting is superior to deny free equity to random hands and build the pot while I likely have the best hand. A small continuation bet size (approx. 1/3 pot) is standard on this texture to keep the opponent's range wide while getting value.

On this Ace-high flop, the board significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor. My hand, Queen-high with no real draw other than a very weak backdoor straight and flush possibility, has almost zero equity against a range that includes many Aces and better pairs. The pot odds of roughly 4:1 aren't enough to justify a call when I am so far behind and out of position. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

In a heads-up situation, while the button (SB) has the advantage of position post-flop, 9-6 offsuit is a relatively weak hand. Given that I am early in the match and the opponent (Pro) showed aggression in the previous hand, I will fold this hand to preserve my stack and avoid playing a marginal hand out of position if they decide to raise from the big blind.

In a heads-up match, the button (Small Blind) opening range should be wide, typically 70-80% of hands, to prevent the Big Blind from realizing equity for free. T5s (Ten-Five suited) is a marginal hand, sitting near the bottom of a playable opening range. While weak, it has flush potential and decent equity against a random hand. A min-raise to 200 risks 150 to win the current 150 pot. Even if the opponent defends reasonably often, the combination of fold equity pre-flop and the advantage of playing in position post-flop makes raising slightly +EV compared to folding. I will open for a standard min-raise.

I am in the Big Blind holding T7o. The button has raised to 200. I have already contributed 100 as the Big Blind, so it costs me 100 more to see a flop. With pot odds of 3:1, T7o is a standard defend against a button open in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. I will call to see the flop.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a relatively dry board (9-7-2 rainbow). As the Big Blind defending against a pre-flop raise, my standard play is to check to the aggressor to see how they react. Middle pair is a solid hand to check-call with if they decide to continuation bet, as the board doesn't have many draws and my hand is likely ahead of their air, while still having some showdown value.

I hold T5s on a dry, rainbow board of 9-7-2. This board texture interacts moderately with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, 9x, 7x, some straight draws like 68/8T), though they also have many missed hands. My hand has very little equity—only Ten-high with backdoor flush potential and no immediate straight draws. While a c-bet could fold out better high-card hands (like K-high or Q-high), T5s functions well as a check-back to realize equity for free. Checking protects my checking range and prevents me from getting blown off my hand by a check-raise. I will take the free card and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a 9-7-2-3 rainbow board. My opponent checked back on the flop, which often indicates they missed or are holding a marginal hand. By checking the turn, I am continuing to control the size of the pot with a medium-strength hand and leaving the door open for my opponent to bluff if they have nothing. There is little reason to bet here, as I likely won't get called by many worse hands, and I want to get to showdown cheaply or induce a bet from a weaker hand.

On the turn, the board is 2c 9d 7s 3d. I hold 5c Tc, giving me Ten-high with no straight or flush draws (only two clubs, no straight connection). The opponent checked to me again. While the opponent has shown weakness, my hand has zero equity if called, as I draw dead against almost any pair and have very few outs against high cards. Bluffing here is inefficient because I block nothing and have no equity backup. It is better to check back, try to realize the small equity of hitting a pair on the river, or simply give up to a river bet. There is no value in bloating the pot with air.

I have Two Pair (Sevens and Nines) with a Ten kicker. The action has been very passive, with both players checking the flop and the turn. This suggests that neither of us has a particularly strong hand. My Seven now plays as part of two pair on this paired board. Checking is the most sensible action to reach showdown cheaply, as I am unlikely to get called by many worse hands if I bet, and I can comfortably call a small to medium-sized bet if my opponent decides to bluff.

On the river, the board is 2c 9d 7s 3d 9h. I hold T5s, leaving me with Ten-high (playing the board's pair of nines with a Ten kicker). My hand has negligible showdown value, losing to any pair and any Jack-high or better. The opponent has checked to me on all three streets, which signifies weakness—likely a weak pair (2x, 3x, 7x), Ace-high, or complete air. However, my own line of checking back the flop and turn also signals weakness. A sudden bet on the river is hard to balance; it doesn't credibly represent a 9 (trips) or an overpair, as those hands usually bet earlier for value. Consequently, the opponent is likely to look me up with any pair or even Ace-high, making a bluff unprofitable. Since I cannot value bet and a bluff is unlikely to succeed often enough to justify the cost, the best play is to check back and surrender the small pot if beaten.

In a heads-up situation, Q8o is a solid hand to open from the button. Raising here allows me to take initiative and leverage my positional advantage throughout the rest of the hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 total.

In Heads-Up play, Pocket Nines (99) is a premium hand, well ahead of the opponent's standard opening range. The opponent opened for 3x (300 chips). While a 3-bet for value is a strong option to deny equity and extract value from lower pairs or broadway cards, playing 99 out of position in a bloated 3-bet pot can be difficult if overcards flop (which happens frequently). Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, including their bluffs, and controls the size of the pot. I will flat call to see the flop and re-evaluate.

I hold Pocket Nines (99) on a low, wet flop of 2h-4h-3d. While I likely have the best hand with an overpair, the board texture connects well with straight draws (5x, Ax) and flush draws. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check. This allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including bluffs and draws. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his air and only get action from hands that have good equity against me. I will check to the aggressor and plan to call or raise depending on his bet size.

On a very low, connected board of 2h-4h-3d, my Qs 8d has overcards but no direct connection. This board texture is generally better for the Big Blind's calling range, which contains more low cards and straight combinations. Since Pro checked to me, I have the option to take a free card. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as a bluff since Pro likely continues with any Ace, five, pair, or heart draw, and my hand has some showdown value against complete air. I will check back to control the pot and see the turn.

The opponent checked back the flop on a 2h-4h-3d board, which suggests they likely do not have a strong overpair or a very strong draw, but rather a marginal hand, Ace-high, or air. The Turn is the Jack of Spades. This card connects well with the opponent's pre-flop raising range (broadway cards like KJ, QJ, AJ) that might have checked back the flop for pot control. While my Pocket Nines are likely still good against hands like A-high or smaller pairs (55-88), betting here risks isolating myself against a Jack or better. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially induce bluffs from missed draws or air, and evaluate the opponent's action on a card that favors their range more than mine.

The turn Jack doesn't change much for my Queen-high. Pro has checked twice, suggesting weakness or a trap on this low-connected board. Since I checked back the flop, checking the turn allows me to see a free river and potentially win at showdown if the board stays dry or if I hit a Queen. Betting into a board that likely hits the Big Blind's range (2-3-4-5-A) without a solid hand or draw is unnecessary when I can take a free card and realize my equity.

The river brings a 6s, making the board 2h-4h-3d-Js-6s. This puts four cards to a straight on the board (2-3-4-6), meaning any 5 makes a straight. The opponent checked back the flop and the turn, which heavily caps their range; they likely do not have a strong overpair, set, or top pair (Jack), as those hands typically bet the turn for value. My Pocket Nines are likely ahead of their showdown value (like 77, 88, or Ace-high), but I cannot confidently value bet because worse hands will likely fold on this scary board, and better hands (straights) will call or raise. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply or potentially induce a bluff from missed heart draws or air. If the opponent bets, I will have to evaluate based on sizing, but checking is the best way to maximize EV here.

The board is 2h-4h-3d-Js-6s. After raising pre-flop and checking through the flop and turn, both players have shown a lack of strength. My Queen-high has a small amount of showdown value in a pot where no one has shown interest. While betting might force a fold from some weak pairs, it is more likely that Pro would call with Ace-high or any small pair that beats me. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown and potentially win the pot without risking more chips on a weak hand.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Small Blind (Button) typically opens a very wide range (often 80%+). However, 65 off-suit falls into the bottom 10-20% of hands (trash hands). While I have position, the hand has poor playability and equity against a calling range. Since we are 100bb deep, there is no desperate need to steal with garbage. Folding here maintains a balanced opening frequency and avoids a marginally -EV situation.

3h 5s is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Being in the Small Blind/Button position, while I have a positional advantage post-flop, the hand lacks the equity or playability to justify a call or a raise against a 'Pro' level opponent. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the Button (SB) with J8o in a heads-up match. With 100bb effective stacks, this hand falls well within a standard opening range (typically top 70-80% of hands). My opponent has played passively so far, rarely re-raising, which increases the value of raising to steal the blind or play a pot in position. I will stick to a standard min-raise size of 2bb to exert pressure while keeping the risk low.

In heads-up play, K8o is a very strong hand to defend from the Big Blind against a standard button open. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 100 to win 300), and my hand has plenty of equity against a wide opening range. I will call to see the flop and play post-flop.

I have middle pair (Kings) on a board that contains an Ace and a King. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as they likely hold many Ace-x and King-x hands. I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. Second pair is a strong enough hand to check-call a standard bet, but leading out (donk betting) here would likely only be called by hands that beat me.

The board texture (Ah Kc 9h) hits the Big Blind's calling range heavily, as it contains many Ax, Kx, and broadway combinations (QJ, QT, JT). Although this board favors my range as the pre-flop raiser, my specific hand (8c Jd) has very poor equity (no pair, very weak backdoor draws). While I could c-bet small representing an Ace or King, the probability of the opponent holding a calling hand is high given the two broadway cards on the board. In Hand #1, the opponent folded to a c-bet on A-8-5, but A-K-9 is much more coordinated and likely to have connected. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize any small equity I have for free, and re-evaluate on the turn without wasting chips on a low-probability bluff.

I have second pair (Kings) with an eight kicker on a board that is becoming increasingly coordinated. Pro checked back on the flop, which might indicate a lack of an Ace or a desire to play a medium-strength hand (like a King or a Nine) cautiously. The Jack of clubs on the turn is a scare card that completes some straights (like Q-T) and improves hands like K-J or A-J. I will check to maintain pot control and see if Pro bets. My hand has good showdown value, but betting now likely only gets called by better hands or draws that have significant equity against me.

I have improved to a pair of Jacks on the Turn (Board: Ah Kc 9h Jc). While this gives me showdown value, the board is incredibly coordinated and 'wet'. The presence of the Ace and King hits the Big Blind's defense range hard, and the Jack connects with Queens and Tens for straights (QT is the nuts). Since my opponent has checked to me again, they likely don't have a monster hand, but betting opens me up to a check-raise or isolates me against better hands (Ax, Kx, Two Pair). Checking back controls the pot size, denies the opponent a check-raise opportunity, and allows me to see the river for free with a hand that has reasonable showdown value but cannot sustain heavy action.

I have second pair (Kings) with an eight kicker. The action has gone check-check on both the flop and the turn. This suggests that Pro likely does not have an Ace and is looking to reach showdown with a marginal hand or has given up on a draw. While my hand is likely the best, betting into this board might only get called by better hands (like KT, KQ, or KJ) or marginal hands that I already beat. By checking, I ensure a showdown and can potentially catch a bluff or win the pot if Pro also checks behind. There is no reason to build a pot with a medium-strength hand on this coordinated board.

I hold 3rd pair (Jacks) on a board of [Ah Kc 9h Jc 3s]. The board is very coordinated with four broadway cards, meaning straights (QT) and two-pair combinations are possible. My opponent has checked to me on the Flop, Turn, and River. This line typically indicates a weak hand, such as a missed draw, a lower pair (9x), or a small pocket pair. While I likely have the best hand, betting for value is thin because worse hands (like 9x or missed draws) will likely fold, and better hands (Ax, Kx) will call. Checking back allows me to win the pot against missed draws and lower pairs without risking chips or facing a check-raise.

Ace-Queen offsuit is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. I have the button (SB) and should raise to build the pot and gain initiative. A 3x open to 300 is a standard sizing to apply pressure on the Big Blind.

I hold AQo, a premium hand in heads-up play, but I am out of position in the Big Blind. The opponent has raised to 3x (300 chips), which they previously did with a strong hand (Pocket Nines). While a 3-bet for value is a valid option to build the pot and seize the initiative, calling is a prudent strategy here for several reasons: 1) It keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing me to dominate weaker Ax or Qx hands that might fold to a 3-bet. 2) It controls the pot size while playing out of position. 3) The larger open size (3x) offers slightly worse odds for a 3-bet bluff/value ratio compared to a min-raise. I will call to see a flop.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a dry, rainbow board (Q-8-2). As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture is not very coordinated, meaning there are few draws I need to deny equity to immediately. Checking allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including bluffs (air) or weaker value hands (lower pairs), whereas leading out (donk betting) might cause them to fold hands I want to keep in the pot or only continue with hands that beat me. My hand is strong enough to call a bet or check-raise later, but for now, checking maximizes the chance to extract value from the opponent's aggressive actions.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-Queen on a very dry board of Queen-Deuce-Eight. Since my opponent checked to me, I should bet for value to extract chips from weaker Queens, middle pairs like pocket tens or nines, and any Eight in the Big Blind's range. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a standard sizing that keeps the pot building while allowing weaker hands to continue.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with AQ on a dry Q-8-2 rainbow board. The opponent, who has been somewhat passive post-flop in previous hands (checking back 99 on the flop in Hand #4), has made a half-pot continuation bet of 300. Given the dry board texture, there are very few draws to protect against. Raising here (check-raising) would likely fold out all of the opponent's bluffs and weaker hands, isolating me against sets or strong two-pair hands, though TPTK is likely best. Calling is the superior option to keep the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing or to value-bet worse Queens (KQ, QJ) on later streets. I am well ahead of their range and want to extract maximum value.

The Turn brings the King of Hearts, which is an overcard to my pair of Queens. This card favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (AK, KQ, KJ) more than mine. My hand (Second Pair, Top Kicker) now shifts from a clear value hand to a marginal made hand or bluff-catcher. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from hands that beat me (Kings, Sets, Two Pair) and fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. By checking, I control the pot size and allow the opponent to potentially bluff with draws (Hearts, Straight draws) or check back with weaker Queens or underpairs (JJ, TT). I hold the Ace of Hearts, which blocks the nut flush, giving me good blockers if facing a bet.

The King on the turn is an overcard to my pair of Queens. While I still have a strong hand with Ace-Queen, checking back here is a prudent pot control move. It prevents me from being check-raised by a hand that has improved to a King or a slow-played set. Checking also allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, potentially inducing a bluff on the river or getting value from a weaker hand that might check-call again.

I hold Second Pair (Queens) with an Ace kicker on a wet board (Qh 2d 8c Kh Jh) that contains three hearts and straight possibilities (T9, AT). My opponent showed strength on the flop but weakness by checking back the Turn (King). The River Jack is a scary card that completes potential straights and flushes. Leading out (betting) is unlikely to get value from worse hands and exposes me to raises from better ones. My hand has showdown value, and I hold the Ace of Hearts (Nut Flush Blocker), which is useful for bluff-catching, but the standard line with a marginal made hand out of position is to check and evaluate.

I have a strong pair of Queens with an Ace kicker. While the board has run out with a King and a Jack, and three hearts are present, my opponent has checked to me on the river. Given the potential for my opponent to have a King, two pair, or a flush, checking back is the most prudent play. I have significant showdown value and betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me or very narrow marginal hands I already beat. Checking ensures I win the pot if my Queens are best without risking a check-raise.

I am on the Button with 6s4s, a suited one-gapper. In heads-up play, this hand is well within the top 80-90% of hands typically played from the button. Given the opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to flat call raises out of the Big Blind but play passively post-flop (checking down hands 3 and 7, folding to a c-bet in hand 1), I prefer raising over limping. Raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or use my positional advantage to c-bet effective flops. If re-raised, I can easily fold.

K7o is a standard defense hand from the big blind against a button raise. I am getting 3-to-1 pot odds to call the additional 100 chips. Given the stack depths and the opponent's previous patterns, calling to see a flop is the most profitable play here.

On this flop of 8d-9s-4d, I have King-high with a gutter-ball straight draw possibility (requiring a 5, 6, 10, or J to improve significantly, though none are immediate). Against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser to see their continuation bet sizing. I have no reason to lead out here with just King-high.

I hold bottom pair (4s) and a gutshot straight draw (need a 7 for 4-6-7-8-9) on a somewhat wet board (8d 9s 4d). While I have showdown value, it is marginal. The board texture connects well with the Big Blind's calling range (8x, 9x, flush draws, straight draws like 56, 67, 7T, etc.). Betting opens me up to check-raises which would force me to fold my equity, or calls from better hands. Checking back controls the pot, allows me to realize my equity (hitting a 7, 4, or 6 on the turn), and induces bluffs from opponent's missed hands on later streets. Given the opponent's history of passivity and checking down marginal hands, checking is the low-variance, optimal play to realize equity.

On the turn 2s, the board remains relatively disconnected. Having checked the flop and seen the pre-flop aggressor check back, it is likely that Pro does not have a monster hand, or they are pot-controlling with a medium-strength hand. With King-high and no real draw, I have very little showdown value, but leading out into a range that checked back often yields little fold equity against pairs. I will check again to see if Pro checks behind or offers information with a bet sizing.

I hold a pair of fours (bottom pair) along with a strong flush draw (holding 6s4s on a board with 9s2s) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Although I have significant equity, my current hand strength is marginal against an opponent who calls widely. Betting here risks getting raised or bloating the pot with the second-best hand, while checking back guarantees I realize my equity for free. If I hit my flush or straight on the river, I can value bet; if not, I can evaluate based on the opponent's action. This passive line minimizes variance and exploits the opponent's tendency to play passively.

The hand has been checked down to the river on a board of 8d-9s-4d-2s-Jh. Pro raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a range of weak pairs, Ace-high, or total air that is looking to realize showdown value or give up. My King-high has some showdown value in this small, checked-down pot. While I could attempt a bluff to push off small pairs, checking is the safest play to see if my King-high is sufficient to win against their missed overcards or draws.

I hold a pair of fours (bottom pair) on a board of 8d-9s-4d-2s-Jh. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn, indicating weakness from both sides. The river Jack is an overcard but doesn't complete the obvious flush or straight draws. My hand beats missed draws (like 5-6, 6-7, or diamond draws) which make up a reasonable portion of the opponent's range given the passive line. Betting achieves little: worse hands will fold, and better hands (any pair of 8s, 9s, Js, or pocket pairs) will likely call. Turning this hand into a bluff is unnecessary and risky against a passive calling station. The optimal play is to check back and take the showdown, hoping my bottom pair is good against the opponent's air.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 6s 3h, which is a very weak hand. In heads-up play, while I should play a wide range from the button, 63o is near the bottom of the range and generally not worth a raise or a call against a competent opponent. Folding is the most disciplined play here to conserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 8h 5d, which is an off-suit one-gapper and a very weak hand, ranking in the bottom 20% of starting hands. Reviewing my previous play, I folded 6c 5h in Hand #5, and 8h 5d is a weaker hand than that. Although my opponent has been passive, playing such a weak hand even in position can lead to marginal spots where I bleed chips. Maintaining a fold frequency for the bottom of my range is optimal here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Hearts and Five of Diamonds (T5o). This is a weak hand, roughly in the bottom 35-40% of starting hands in heads-up play. Looking at my previous history in this match, I have consistently folded hands of similar strength (96o in Hand 2, 35s in Hand 6, 63o in Hand 10) while raising with stronger holdings like Q8o and AQo. Maintaining this strategy keeps my range relatively defined and avoids playing mediocre hands out of position or in bloated pots against 'Pro'. Given the current stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament, folding is a safe, disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 9d 5c, a very weak off-suit unconnected hand (95o). At 100bb deep, this hand falls well below the standard opening range for heads-up play. While I have position post-flop, the hand has poor playability and equity. In previous hands (Hand 5 and 11), I have folded similar weak holdings (65o, 58o) from this position. There is no reason to deviate here; preserving chips for better spots is the optimal strategy.

I am on the Button/Small Blind with Qh 3d. This is a relatively weak hand, but in heads-up play, widening the opening range is standard. The opponent (Pro) has been fairly active. Raising here is an option to steal the blinds, but folding or calling are also viable. Given the stack depths and the weakness of 3-offsuit, I will fold to maintain my stack and wait for a better spot to engage, as I have been folding or playing cautiously in previous hands.

I hold 5d 6h, a weak off-suit connector, on the Button. In previous hands (Hand #5 with 6c 5h and Hand #11 with 5d 8h), I have folded similar weak holdings. The opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind frequently against my raises (calling in Hands 1, 3, 7, and 9), which reduces the profitability of raising as a bluff with weak cards. Playing a weak hand like 65o against a wide calling range often leads to difficult post-flop situations even with position. Therefore, folding is the prudent low-variance play consistent with my previous actions.
In heads-up play, 56o is a reasonable hand to open from the Small Blind to maintain aggression and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I will raise to 300 to apply pressure on the Big Blind.

Opponent opens to 300 (3BB) from the Button/SB. I hold K7s in the Big Blind. This hand is well within the standard defense range in heads-up play, as it has good equity against a wide button opening range and decent playability post-flop. I am getting appropriate pot odds to call. Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is not necessary given the hand strength and stack depth. I will call to see a flop.

I hold the second nut flush draw (Ks 7s) on a board of 2s 4s 9h. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see how the opponent proceeds. If the opponent bets, I have excellent equity to continue, either by calling or check-raising as a semi-bluff. Leading out (donk betting) is unnecessary and balances my checking range poorly.

I have a gutshot straight draw needing a 3 to complete a straight on a 2s-4s-9h board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have no showdown value with 6-high, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure and attempt to win the hand immediately.

I hold the second nut flush draw with an overcard on a board of 2s 4s 9h. Against a standard continuation bet of half the pot, I have significant equity (likely 40-50% against a wide range). Folding is incorrect given the pot odds and hand strength. While a check-raise is a viable aggressive option to generate fold equity or build a pot, calling is the standard play here to keep the opponent's range wide, keep their bluffs in, and control the size of the pot while out of position. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold the second nut flush draw (Ks 7s) on a board of 2s 4s 9h 3d. The turn card (3d) connects the low cards but doesn't complete my flush. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who c-bet the flop, the standard line is to check. This allows the opponent to continue bluffing or check back, giving me a free look at the river. Leading out (donk betting) is not optimal as it folds out his bluffs and isolates me against better hands. I will check and evaluate based on his action.

I have successfully turned the nut straight (2-3-4-5-6). On the flop, I had a gutshot straight draw which the 3d on the turn completed. Since Pro has checked, I will lead with a value bet of 800 into the 1200 pot. This sizing is designed to extract maximum value from hands like top pair (9x), two pair, or sets, while also charging any flush draws (spades) that might be looking for a river card.

I hold a King-high flush draw (Ks7s) on a board of 2s 4s 9h 3d. The turn card (3d) does not complete my flush but adds a low connected texture to the board. My opponent has fired a second barrel of 800 into a pot of 1200 (66% pot). I have 9 spade outs to a flush, and potentially 3 King outs to top pair, giving me approximately 12 outs (~26% equity). The pot odds required to call are 28.5% (800 to call into a total pot of 2800). Although my direct immediate equity is slightly below the required odds, the difference is small enough to be covered by implied odds (the ability to win a larger pot if I hit my flush on the river). Additionally, my King-high currently beats some of his semi-bluffs (like lower flush draws). Folding a strong draw like the second nut flush draw would be too tight in this spot.

I hold King-high (Ks7s) on a final board of 2s 4s 9h 3d 6d. I missed my flush draw. The river 6d puts a potential 4-card straight on the board (2-3-4-6), meaning any 5 makes a straight. Leading out (donk betting) would accomplish little, as it likely folds out worse hands and builds a pot against a stronger range. The standard and optimal play is to check to the aggressor. If faced with a bet, I will likely have to fold, but checking gives the possibility of seeing a free turn card.

The board is 5d Qc Qh. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on this dry, paired board as it is more likely for the button to hold a Queen or high pocket pairs than the Big Blind. Pro checked to me, which is expected. I will place a continuation bet of 200 (1/3 pot) to take advantage of this texture and attempt to win the pot immediately. It's difficult for Pro to continue here without a Queen, a pocket pair, or a Five.

I hold 9c6c on a board of 5d-Qc-Qh. I have no pair and no immediate draws (only weak backdoor flush and straight possibilities). The opponent has c-bet 200 into a pot of 600 (1/3 pot) on a paired board that favors their pre-flop raising range. While the price to call is attractive (getting 4:1 odds), my hand has very little equity and poor playability out of position. Continuing here would be a losing play in the long run against a standard c-betting range. The disciplined decision is to fold.

I hold a pocket pair of Threes (33) on the Button (SB). In heads-up poker, any pocket pair is a respectable hand, typically ahead of a random hand. I have the positional advantage post-flop. Raising is the standard play to assert aggression, potentially take down the blinds immediately, or build a pot where I have position. Limping is an option to keep the pot small, but raising denies free equity to the Big Blind's random holdings (like 72o or J4o) which have live cards against me. Given the opponent has 3-bet me previously, a min-raise minimizes my loss if I am forced to fold to a re-raise while still accomplishing the goal of stealing or value betting.

I am in the big blind with Js3d, a relatively weak holding. Pro has raised to 200 from the small blind. I need to call 100 more to see a flop with a pot of 300. While the pot odds are 3-to-1, this hand performs poorly post-flop and Pro has been aggressive in several previous hands. However, in a heads-up scenario, folding to a single min-raise is often too tight. I will call to defend my blind and see if I can connect with the flop, though I plan to play cautiously.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack, although my kicker is very weak. The board is Jack of Clubs, Eight of Clubs, and Five of Hearts. This board has some straight and flush draw possibilities. Pro checked to me, and since I am the Big Blind and was the caller pre-flop, I have the option to check back or bet. Given that I have top pair but a weak kicker, checking back is a safe play to control the pot size and avoid getting blown off the hand by a check-raise. It also allows Pro to potentially bluff on the turn with weaker hands. I will check to see the turn for free and maintain pot control.

I hold pocket Threes (33) on a Jc-8c-5h board. I have position on the Button. This is a low pocket pair on a board that likely connects with the Big Blind's calling range (Jacks, Eights, Fives, club flush draws, and straight draws like 6-7 or 9-10). Betting here turns my hand into a bluff essentially, as worse hands (unpaired overcards) might fold, but better hands (any pair) will call. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs or catch a favorable turn card cheaply. I want to get to showdown as cheaply as possible with this marginal showdown value.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker on a board of Jc-8c-5h-Th. In the previous street (flop), I checked and Pro checked back, which suggests Pro likely does not have a premium hand like AJ, KJ, or a set. The Ten on the turn is a somewhat coordinated card, but since I am out of position, I will continue to play cautiously. Checking again allows me to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from Pro if they are on a draw or holding air. If Pro bets, I have a clear call given the strength of my top pair relative to the range they would check back on the flop.

On the Turn, the board is Jc 8c 5h Th. I hold a pair of Threes (33). The Ten of Hearts is an overcard to my pair and brings additional straight and flush draws (hearts). Flash checks again, signaling either weakness or a draw. My hand has very little value; almost any pair beats me, and the board is coordinated enough that Flash likely connected with a piece of it or has a draw. Betting turns my hand into a bluff that is unlikely to fold out better hands (like an 8 or 5) and risks getting check-raised. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a river card, and potentially get to showdown cheaply against missed draws or high-card hands.

I have top pair (Jacks) on a board that has now paired the Eights, giving me two pair with a very weak Trey kicker. Pro has checked back on both the flop and the turn after being the pre-flop aggressor. This passivity suggests Pro likely has a weak-to-medium strength hand or has completely missed the board. There is little value in betting here, as most hands that would call probably have me beaten (like a Jack with a better kicker or a Ten), while hands I beat will likely fold. By checking, I can reach a cheap showdown and potentially induce a bluff from a missed draw or Ace-high. I will check and plan to call a standard-sized bet.

On the River, the board is [Jc 8c 5h Th 8h]. I hold [3h 3s]. My pair of Threes is counterfeited by the board (J-T-8-8-5), meaning I am essentially 'playing the board'. I lose to any opponent holding a card rank 6 or higher, and any pocket pair 66 or higher. I only chop against hands composed entirely of cards 5 or lower (e.g., 4-2, 22). The opponent has checked all three streets, indicating weakness or a draw (likely missed clubs). However, given the opponent's tendency to check/call (passive station), trying to bluff them off a better hand (like Ace-high or King-high which beats the board) in a small pot of 400 is risky and likely negative EV. It is better to check back and surrender the pot if beaten, rather than losing additional chips in a failed bluff.

I am on the button with 5h-6d. While this is a marginal hand, in heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is important. Pro has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop raises in recent hands (Hands 18, 24). I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds or take control of the hand early.

I hold a pair of Deuces (2d, 2h) in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 from the Button. Based on previous hands (e.g., Hand #24 with 23s, Hand #36 with 69s), Flash opens a wide range of hands to 300. I am getting 2:1 odds on a call (200 to win 400), requiring 33% equity. Pocket Deuces has approximately 48-50% equity against a wide opening range. While 22 is vulnerable post-flop, the price is too good to fold heads-up. 3-betting is less attractive as it bloats the pot with a hand that is difficult to navigate without improving to a set. Therefore, calling is the most standard and profitable line.

I hold a pair of Deuces (2d, 2h) on a flop of Ks-8s-6s. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the aggressor is the standard play. The board favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Kings, broadways), and my hand has very low showdown value and practically no draw equity. Leading out serves no purpose as it folds out bluffs and isolates me against better hands. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's subsequent action.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a flop of 6s-8s-Kc. While I have a piece of the board, my kicker is weak and the board is somewhat coordinated with a flush draw and an overcard (King). Pro has checked to me. In heads-up play, checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot and realize my equity. Betting might force out hands I beat while getting called by hands that dominate me (like a King, an 8, or a better 6). By checking, I can see a turn card for free and potentially reach showdown or improve to two pair or trips.

On the turn, the board reads 6s-8s-Kc-9d. My pair of Deuces is an underpair and currently very weak. My opponent checked back the flop, which often signals a hand with some showdown value (like middle/bottom pair or Ace high) or a give-up, though it could be a pot-control line with a weak King. Leading out here turns my hand into a bluff, and it's unlikely to fold out better hands like an 8, 6, or pocket pair higher than 22. Checking allows me to potentially see a free river or evaluate if the opponent decides to bluff. I will check/fold to a bet.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a board of 6s-8s-Kc-9d. I checked back the flop to control the pot size and realize equity. The 9 on the turn is not a great card for me as it connects the board further, but Pro has checked for a second time. While I might have the best hand, betting is unlikely to get a better hand to fold, and I'm likely only to be called by an 8, a 9, a King, or a straight draw. Checking back again allows me to get to a cheap showdown or see if the river changes the board texture.

The river is the 4h. The board is [6s 8s Kc 9d 4h]. I hold pocket Deuces (22). My opponent, Flash, raised pre-flop but has checked back both the flop and the turn. 

Looking at history (specifically Hand #28), Flash has shown a tendency to check back middle strength hands (like second pair) on the turn and river to get to showdown cheaply. In this hand, checking back the flop on a King-high board suggests he likely doesn't have a strong King, but his range likely contains Ace-highs, pocket pairs (33-77), or hands that hit the 6, 8, or 9. My 22 is currently an underpair to the board and likely has very little showdown value against a range that includes Ace-highs and any pair. 

However, because Flash has demonstrated passivity with made hands (checking back pairs), bluffing is risky as he is likely to call with the hands I am trying to fold out (e.g., a pair of 6s or Ace-high). Checking allows me to see a free showdown if he checks back, or fold if he bets, minimizing losses. There is little value in betting as a bluff given his tendency to call/check down marginal hands.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a board of 6s-8s-Kc-9d-4h. The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn, and now Pro has checked the river as well. This suggests Pro has a weak hand or a missed draw. While my pair of sixes is not particularly strong given the three overcards (K, 9, 8) on the board, it holds significant showdown value in a heads-up pot where no bets have been made since pre-flop. Betting here is unlikely to get better hands (like an 8, a 9, or a King) to fold, and worse hands are unlikely to call. Therefore, checking back to reach showdown for free is the most profitable and safest play.

I hold King-Ace offsuit (AKo) on the button, which is a premium starting hand. I am in the Big Blind with Qh 4c and the button (Pro) has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. I have middle pair (Queens) on a board featuring a King. While I have a decent hand for heads-up play, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the most standard and balanced play here. This allows me to control the pot size and gather more information based on Pro's reaction.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a board with a flush draw (two hearts) and straight possibilities (Q-K connectivity). I have middle pair (Queens) with a 4 kicker on a board of Kh-Qc-6h. The opponent (Pro) is the pre-flop aggressor and has made a continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn develops.

The turn Ten is a potentially dangerous card as it completes straight draws like AJ or J9. Checking is the most prudent move to control the pot size and see a river card for free.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AK on K-Q-T-6 board). The Turn card (Ten of Clubs) is somewhat dangerous as it completes potential straights (J9, AJ) and makes two-pair combinations like KT or QT possible for the Big Blind defender. In Hand #35, I faced a similar connected board with Aces and chose to check the turn for pot control. Checking back here allows me to realize my equity, potentially hit a straight on the river (Jack), and keep the pot manageable. I can comfortably call a river bet or value bet if checked to on a safe river.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a very weak kicker. The board is now extremely coordinated; any Jack makes a straight, and the backdoor club flush has also come home. After my opponent checked behind on the turn, they likely have a marginal King, a Queen with a better kicker, or a hand like a Ten. There is no value in betting here because I will almost never be called by a worse hand, and I'm not turning a pair of Queens into a bluff. Checking is the only sensible option to try and reach a cheap showdown.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AK) on a very dangerous board reading 6h-Qc-Kh-Tc-9c. The river 9c puts four cards to a straight (9-T-Q-K) on the board, meaning any Jack makes a straight. However, betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like Qx or underpairs) given the scary board texture, and will only be called by hands that beat me (Two Pair, Straights, Flushes). I have sufficient showdown value to win against missed draws or worse Kings, so taking the free showdown is the optimal play.

I have been dealt pocket sevens on the button. This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I will follow my standard opening pattern of raising to 300 chips to build the pot and put pressure on the big blind while maintaining a consistent betting strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). My hand is strong enough to defend against a wide button opening range. The flop comes 3s 5h Td, which is a fairly dry and disconnected board. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, I am out of position against the aggressor. I have pocket sevens, which is an overpair to the two low cards on the board (3 and 5), though I am behind any Ten. Pro checked to me, which is a standard play for the Big Blind. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to extract value from weaker hands like smaller pairs (44, 3x, 5x) or overcards that might call, and to protect my hand from giving a free card to hands like KQ or AJ.

I am out of position with Queen-Jack offsuit on a Ten-high dry board (3s 5h Td). My opponent, Flash, has c-bet 300 (half pot). While QJo holds two overcards, my equity is marginal (roughly 25-35% against a wide button range), and playing overcards out of position is difficult, especially against an opponent who has shown the ability to fire multiple barrels (Hand #16) or check down (Hand #26, #28). The price of 300 requires roughly 25% equity to call, but realizing that equity is tough. Without a pair or a strong draw, and facing a half-pot bet rather than a small continuation bet, the disciplined play is to fold and preserve the stack.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Kh 4h. In heads-up play, a suited King is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. I have consistently been opening to 200 (min-raise) in previous hands, so maintaining this sizing balances my range. Folding is too tight given the hand strength, and raising is generally superior to limping to take initiative and play in position.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Pro has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, 7-2 offsuit performs very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I have a lead in the tournament and there is no need to defend with the absolute bottom of my range, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with suited connectors (4d 3d). This is a good hand to play aggressively in position to either take the blinds now or set up a play post-flop. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind.

Opponent Flash raises to 300 (3bb) from the Button/SB. This 3x sizing offers worse pot odds (calling 200 to win 400, needing 33% equity) compared to a min-raise. While T4s has raw equity close to 37-40% against a wide button opening range (Flash has shown hands like 23s, 69s, 2Js), playing a weak suited gapper Out of Position (OOP) against an aggressive opponent who is capable of multi-street barreling (as seen in Hand #16) is difficult. The reverse implied odds and the difficulty of realizing equity OOP make this a fold against a 3bb open.

I hold A9s on the button (SB), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play, typically well within the top range of hands. In heads-up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button acts first pre-flop but has the advantage of acting last post-flop. Given my hand strength and position, raising is the standard and most profitable play to build the pot and define ranges. I will use my standard opening size of a min-raise to 200. This balances my range and puts pressure on the Big Blind, while allowing me to play a pot in position with a hand that has good playability and high equity.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, any King-high hand is generally strong enough to defend against a standard open, especially when getting such a good price. I will call and see a flop out of position.

I am out of position with King-high on a 9-5-4 board with two spades. This board doesn't particularly favor my range over the pre-flop raiser's, and I have not connected with it. I have flopped Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a board with a flush draw and straight connectivity ([4s 5d 9s]). In heads-up play, this is a very strong hand. A half-pot bet size of 200 balances getting value while not folding out all worse hands, consistent with my sizing in previous hands where I received calls.

On a flop of 9-5-4, my King-Trey offsuit has almost no equity against a standard continuation bet range. While I have one overcard (the King), I have no flush draw and only a very weak backdoor straight draw. I am out of position, and the 200-chip bet from Pro represents half the pot. Given my position and the lack of connectivity with this board, calling here would likely lead to a difficult decision on the turn without much improvement. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I have been dealt pocket fives (5c, 5s) on the button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well ahead of an average random hand. I want to build a pot and take the initiative as the aggressor. Standard opening size in this match has been 300, which puts pressure on the opponent while keeping the pot manageable if they defend. I will raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with As3d. The opponent, who has been raising consistently to 3x (300 chips) from the Button, opens again. With 91 BBs effective, I am deep enough to play post-flop. I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker (As3d) on a dry, rainbow board of [6s 3h Td]. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check. My hand has showdown value against their missed overcards (like KQ, QJ) and I want to keep their bluffing range wide. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out worse hands and isolate me against better ones. I will check and evaluate, intending to call a standard continuation bet.

I have a medium pocket pair on a relatively dry board. The Ten is an overcard, but the 6 and 3 are lower than my pair. Pro checked to me, and since I have the initiative from pre-flop, a continuation bet is standard here to protect my hand from overcards and take down the pot immediately. In a similar previous hand (Hand 40) with pocket sevens on a 3-5-T board, a half-pot bet was successful. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot.

I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker (As3d) on a dry T-6-3 rainbow board. My opponent, Flash, has c-bet 300 (half-pot) after raising pre-flop from the button. In a heads-up match, button opening ranges are wide, and c-betting frequencies are often high. My hand beats all missed overcards (AK, AQ, KQ, etc.) and pure bluffs. I am getting pot odds of 25% (300 to win 1200), and my equity against a standard c-betting range is significantly higher than that. Folding bottom pair here would be too tight and exploitable. Raising would likely only get action from better hands. Therefore, the correct play is to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Threes) on the turn with the arrival of the Ace of Clubs. The board is now 6s 3h Td Ac. The Ace is a significant card that hits the opponent's perceived range (Button opening range) quite hard. If the opponent has a strong Ace (AK, AQ, AJ), they will likely bet for value if I check. Leading out (donk betting) here would likely fold out their air and might only get called by hands that beat me or strong draws, though the board is dry. Checking allows me to extract value from aggressive bluffs or worse value bets while disguising the strength of my hand. I plan to check-call or potentially check-raise depending on the bet size.

The turn card is an Ace, which is a significant scare card. While the Ace is generally better for the pre-flop aggressor's range, Pro called my flop continuation bet, suggesting they have a piece of the board (like a Ten or a Six) or a draw. My pocket fives have some showdown value but are now behind any Ten, Six, or the newly arrived Ace. By checking back, I control the pot size and take a free card to the river, where I can re-evaluate based on the final board and Pro's action.

I arrived at the river with two pair (Aces and Threes), but the river 6d counterfeits my lower pair. The board is now [6s 3h Td Ac 6d], giving me Aces and Sixes with a Ten kicker. This is a significant downgrade; previously I beat all single-pair Aces, but now I lose to any Ace with a Jack kicker or better (AJ+) and split with weaker Aces (A2-A9). The opponent checked back the turn when the Ace hit, which usually indicates a weak Ace, a Ten, a pocket pair, or air, rather than a strong Ace. Since my hand has showdown value but cannot get value from worse hands easily (and loses to better Aces which might now value bet), leading out is not profitable. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply or evaluate if the opponent decides to bluff or value bet thin. The goal is to realize my equity without inflating the pot with a hand that has become a 'bluff-catcher' or a 'chop-hand'.

On the river, the board has paired sixes to go with an Ace and a Ten. I have pocket fives, giving me two pair (66 and 55 with an Ace kicker). Pro has checked to me for the third consecutive street. While my hand has some showdown value, it is unlikely to be the best hand if Pro calls a bet, as any Ten, Ace, or Six beats me. By checking back, I minimize my losses against a better hand and realize my showdown value against missed draws or lower pocket pairs.

I am in the Small Blind with J6o. This is a weak hand, falling within the bottom 40% of starting hands in heads-up play. In heads-up play, I have the button and a positional advantage. While 8-5 offsuit is a weak hand, I want to maintain a high level of aggression to keep the pressure on the Big Blind. I hold 2c 5d (5-2 offsuit), which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. My opponent 'Flash' has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. I hold J2o on the Button (SB). This is a very weak hand, typically in the bottom 20% of the range. I am on the button with 8-6 offsuit. While not a premium hand, it's a standard opening candidate in heads-up play, especially against an opponent like Pro who has been folding frequently pre-flop in recent hands. I will raise to my standard 300 to exert pressure and attempt to win the blinds or take control of the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with KJo, facing a 3BB open (300) from the Button. KJo is a strong heads-up hand, well above the median, and has roughly 55-60% equity against a standard button opening range. I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Jack kicker on a rainbow board of Qc Ks 7d. This is a strong hand heads-up. On the flop of Qc-Ks-7d, I have 8-high and no real draws. Pro checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I hold middle pair (Tens) on a wet board [Qc Td 3c] featuring a flush draw and straight possibilities. The pot is 400. This action aligns with a pot-control strategy for marginal made hands in position.

On the turn 3d, the board pairs, but it doesn't significantly change the texture for the pre-flop aggressor who checked back on the flop. This allows me to take the initiative, potentially fold out better high-card hands like AK or AJ, and build a pot in case I hit one of my many outs on the river.

I hold second pair (Tens) on a board of [Qc Td 3c 3d]. After checking back the flop for pot control, I induced a lead from the opponent on the turn. The opponent bets 200 into 400 (half pot), offering me 3:1 pot odds. My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet, as I beat all semi-bluffs (draws) and complete bluffs, and only trail top pair or trips. Raising would likely only get called by better hands. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, including draws and bluffs I currently beat.

The river card is the Jack of Spades, which gives me a pair of Jacks. While this is an improvement over my previous high-card status, the board is now very coordinated (Q-T-3-3-J). The Jack completes potential straights for hands like AK, K9, and 98. A pair of Jacks has some showdown value, but betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and might lead to a difficult decision if raised. Checking is the most prudent play to see a showdown or to potentially catch a bluff from a missed draw.

I hold a pair of Tens (Ts8s) on a board of [Qc Td 3c 3d Js]. The river Jack is an unfavorable card, as it improves any Jx hands to a higher pair than mine and completes straight draws (like K9). My hand has showdown value against the missed draws (clubs/diamonds) but is unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet (missed draws fold, better hands call). Therefore, there is no value in betting, and turning my hand into a bluff is unnecessary as I likely win against their air range. The optimal play is to check back and take the showdown.

I am on the button with 8h 6h, a suited one-gapper that plays well in heads-up situations. My strategy is to maintain aggression from the button and put pressure on the big blind. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300.

Opponent Flash is opening to 300 (3bb) extremely frequently (nearly 100% range observed in recent hands), including weak holdings. I hold Ace-high (Ah9h) on a K-8-3 board with two diamonds. I defended the Big Blind against a frequent 3x open. On a board of Kd-8s-3d, I have middle pair with an eight. The turn is the 8c, making the board Kd 8s 3d 8c. I have improved to three-of-a-kind on the turn. After checking back the flop for pot control, the second eight on the board is a great card for my hand. Pre-flop, I called a 3x raise from the Big Blind with Ah9h. The flop came Kd 8s 3d, which misses my hand but hits the opponent's range (Kings). We both checked, which is standard; the opponent checking back a King on a flush-draw board is possible but less likely than checking back weak pairs or air. The turn is the 8c, pairing the board. Calling here out of position with just Ace-high requires a specific read that the opponent is over-bluffing this specific line with huge frequency. Without that confirmation, and facing a substantial 2/3 pot bet, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot, preserving chips.

I hold Queen-Four offsuit (Q4o) on the Button. While I have position, this is a very weak hand (bottom 25% of range). J8o is a solid hand to open from the button in heads-up play. Pro has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop pressure in previous hands. I will stick with my standard opening raise size of 300 to take control of the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 4s, a very weak offsuit connector. The opponent (Flash) on the Button has raised to 300 (3BB). I need to call 200 to win a pot of 600, offering 33% pot odds. In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, Q7o is a playable hand from the button (SB), typically falling within the opening range. I am effectively 100BB deep. I am in the Big Blind with a strong suited-connector hand (Qh Th). The opponent has raised to 200 from the Button. Based on previous hands (#17, #59, #69), 3-betting to 600 has been highly effective in either taking the pot down immediately or setting up a winning post-flop situation against Pro's wide opening range. I am holding Q7o on the button. I opened to 200, and the opponent (Flash) 3-bet to 600. In my pre-flop plan, I explicitly stated 'I will fold to a 3-bet' with this specific hand. I am on the button with Jack-Eight of clubs, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and take control of the pot early.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with King-high (Kd 9c) on an Ace-high board (3c Ad 4c). This board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, as they have all the strong Aces. On a flop of 3c-Ad-4c, I have a flush draw with Jc8c. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage on an Ace-high board. I hold Kd9c on a board of 3c-Ad-4c. I checked to the pre-flop aggressor, and my opponent bet 300 (half-pot). I hold KJo on the button (SB), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play, well within the top 15-20% of hands. I am in position. I am holding 6c 7d in the Big Blind. Pro raised to 200 from the Button. The cost to call is only 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 4:1 odds. While 7-6 offsuit is not a premium hand, it has decent connectivity for a heads-up match and the pot odds make it a standard call to see a flop.

On a paired board of Qc-8h-8d, I have no connection with my 7d-6c. As the Big Blind caller, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Pro bets, I will likely have to fold given the lack of equity, but checking allows for the possibility of seeing a free turn if they check behind.

On a flop of [Qc 8h 8d], the board is paired and fairly dry. I have turned a pair of sixes on a board of Qc-8h-8d-6h. Pre-flop, Pro raised and then checked behind on the flop, which suggests they likely don't have a Queen or an Eight. The board is Qc 8h 8d 6h. I have King-high (Kd Js). My opponent checked to me on the turn after checking the flop. On a board of Qc-8h-8d-6h-5d, I have a pair of sixes. The board runs out [Qc 8h 8d 6h 5d]. The action has gone check-check on both the flop and turn, and the opponent checks the river again. I hold KdJs (King-high). I am holding pocket sixes on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand that is well ahead of a random range. Consistent with my previous opening raises, I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot.

On a J-7-2 flop with a flush draw, pocket Nines is a marginal made hand (second pair). As the pre-flop caller playing out of position, the standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. The turn brings the Queen of Spades (Qs), putting two overcards to my pocket Nines on the board (Jack and Queen). This card hits the opponent's broadway range quite hard (AQ, KQ, QJ, QT). The Queen of Spades on the turn is an unfavorable card for my pocket sixes, as it is another overcard that likely hits Pro's range or improves their drawing hands. The river card is the Three of Clubs, which completes a potential flush. After I checked the turn, Pro has checked the river as well. I am on the Button (SB) with A9o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the median. Defending the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit against a standard 2x open raise. The pot odds are favorable (100 to win 300), requiring only 25% equity to make calling profitable. While Q7o is a marginal hand, it is strong enough to see a flop heads-up at these stack depths.

I am checking to the pre-flop aggressor. On this 8-5-6 board, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4 or a 9) and one overcard with my Queen. This board texture often favors the Big Blind's range, but standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their continuation bet.

I hold A9o on a flop of 8-6-5. The turn 7s completes a four-to-a-straight board (5-6-7-8). Anyone holding a 9 or a 4 now has a straight. I have a pair of sevens and a gutshot to the higher straight (needing a 9). The turn card is the Seven of Spades (7s), which makes the board 8s-5d-6c-7s. This is a very dynamic board with a 4-card straight (5-6-7-8). Holding A9o, I have the Nine-high straight (5-6-7-8-9). Pro checked the flop but is now leading out on a very coordinated turn card (7s) which completes several straights (any 4 or 9). I have a pair of 7s on a highly coordinated board with four cards to a straight (5-6-7-8) and three spades. Given the action, I am first to act on the river. I hold a Nine-high straight (5-6-7-8-9) on a board of 8s-5d-6c-7s-2s. K5o is a playable hand in heads-up from the Small Blind. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop play.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit (A5o), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot. My previous raises have typically been to 300, so I will maintain that sizing for consistency.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3bb open from the Button (Flash). My hand is K9o, which is a solid defending hand in heads-up play. On this highly connected flop of 8-T-J, my Ace-high has some showdown value but is unlikely to get many folds from better hands if I bet. On the turn, the board pairs with the Eight of Spades (8s), making the board [8d, Ts, Jc, 8s]. My hand (Kd, 9s) retains its open-ended straight draw (needs a Queen or 7) and King-high showdown value. I have Ace-high with some showdown value on a paired, coordinated board. Pro has checked twice, indicating weakness or a trap. Since I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a Jack or Ten) with one bet, and I'd rather not get check-raised, I will check back to see the river for free. On a river board of [8d Ts Jc 8s 7c], my [Kd 9s] makes a Jack-high straight (7-8-9-10-J). The opponent checked back both the flop and turn, suggesting a capped range likely consisting of marginal made hands or missed overcards rather than monsters. 1.  **Hand Strength**: I possess the strongest possible high-card hand. I defeat any hand that lacks a club and lacks a pair (e.g., KhJs without a club). I lose to any pair or any flush.
2.  **Value Betting**: If I bet, worse hands (K-high, J-high air) will simply fold. Better hands (any pair, any flush) will likely call or raise. Thus, there is no value in betting.
3.  **Bluffing**: It is unlikely I can fold out a flush or a strong pair given the pot size and action, though a weak pair might fold. However, turning the best high card into a bluff is generally unnecessary in this spot.
4.  **Showdown Value**: Since the opponent checked all streets, their range is weak. There is a reasonable probability my Ace-high is good against their missed holdings. Taking the free showdown guarantees I realize this equity without risking chips against a trapped flush or a check-calling pair.

Conclusion: Checking back maximizes EV by realizing showdown value and avoiding a situation where I am only called when beaten.
